February 15, 1984 
Statements of Economic I nte.est 
322-6444 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-83-279 
Dear Mr. Burris: 
This letter is sent in response to your request for advice 
from this office on behalf of F. Susan Harrison, the Deputy 
Director of the Sacramento County Department of Airports, 
concerning the conflict of interest provisions of the Political 
Reform Act.ll 
FACTUAL BACKGROUND21 
Ms. Harrison's husband is employed as a pilot by Western 
Airlines. 
As a benefit of his job, Ms. Harrison and her husband 
receive discounted airline travel on Western and other 
airlines. 
In addition, her husband participates in an employee 
stock sharing program. 
Ms. Harrison'S job duties as the Deputy Director for Finance 
and Administration include: 
1. 
Preparing the Department's budget. 
2. 
Analyzing and recommending changes to airport rates 
and charges (e.g., rates for T-hangars and tie-downs). 
11 
California Government Code Sections 81000-91014. 
All 
statutory references are to the California Government Code. 
11 
These facts come from our telephone conversations, 
Ms. Harrison's memo to your office dated June 9, 1983, your 
letters to me dated December 13, 1983 and January 9, 1984, and 
the materials included with your letter. 


J. Steven Burris 
February 15, 1984 
Page 2 
3. 
Negotiating tenant leases at both Sacramento 
Metropolitan and Executive Airports. 
4. 
Administering the air trade development program 
which is a marketing program designed to promote use of 
Me tr 0 Airport •. 
My understanding is that you have already advised 
Ms. Harrison with respect to the setting of airport rates and 
charges. 
Your specific question to us is whether Ms. Harrison 
would have a conflict of interest in negotiating the airline 
leases at Sacramento Metropolitan Airport.l/ 
The County of Sacramento currently has an "Airline Operating 
Agreement and Terminal Building Lease" (the "Agreement") with 
the airlines for use of Sacramento Metropolitan Airport {"Metro 
Airport"}. 
This Agreement was negotiated by Ms. Harrison's 
predecessor and became effective in June 1983; it is a five-year 
Agreement. 
The negotiations for the Agreement are conducted 
between the Deputy Director and a group of airline 
representatives. 
The County is required to treat the airlines 
in an identical manner.if 
The County policy on the operation of Metro Airport is that 
it must be self-supporting. 
Accordingly, in negotiating the 
Agreement, the Deputy Director (Ms. Harrison) begins with the 
total cost of the airport operation, subtracts the amounts from 
revenue sources other than the airlines,1/ and arrives at the 
amount which must be recovered from the airlines. 
The airlines 
are charged for their use of the airport in two ways. 
One 
charge is the landing strip usage fee which is set at a certain 
l/ Both of these questions raise the issue of the 
possible application of Section 1090. 
This office renders 
advice only on the provisions of the Political Reform Act; 
questions concerning Section 1090 should be directed to the 
Attorney General's office. 
i/ The County receives aid from the Airport Development 
Air Program under the jurisdiction of the Federal Aviation 
Administration. 
A condition on receiving this aid is that all 
of the carriers must be treated alike in operation and lease 
agreements. 
i/ Other revenue sources include the hotel ,and gift shop, 
car rental and taxi concessions and other miscellaneous sources. 


J. Steven Burris 
February 15, 1984 
Page 3 
cost per ton of landing weight utilized by the airline. 
The 
other charge is a cost per square foot of space used by the 
airline in the terminal. 
The total which is charged the 
airlines must add up with the other revenues to the amount 
originally set as the total cost for airport operations. 
Even 
though this formula is used, there is still discretion in the 
setting of the charges and in spelling out the other terms of 
the Agreement. 
In addition to participating in the negotiations for the 
Agreement itself, the Deputy Director is responsible for annual 
modifications of the Agreement. 
These modifications to the 
charges are done pursuant to a formula in the Agreement. 
ANALYSIS 
As you know, Section 87100 prohibits a public official from· 
making or participating in governmental decisions in which the 
official has a financial interest in the outcome. 
Section 87103 
defines a financial interest as a reasonably foreseeable 
material financial effect on: 
(a) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect investment worth more 
than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(b) 
Any real property in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect interest worth more 
than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than loans by a 
commercial lending institution in the regular course of 
business on terms available to the public without 
regard to official status, aggregating two hundred 
fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, 
received by or promised to the public official within 
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. 
(d) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. 
(Section 87103 (a) - (d) .) 
Investments include investments of a spouse, and income includes 
income to a spouse. 
Sections 82030 and 82034. !Thus, Western 
Airlines is a source of income to Ms. Harrison and a business 


J. Steven Burris 
February 15, 1984 
Page 4 
entity in which she has an investment. 
Accordingly, 
Ms. Harrison may not make or participate in decisions which 
could have a material financial effect on Western Airlines, 
distinguishable from their effect on the public generally. 
It is reasonably foreseeable that decisions concerning the 
airline leases at Metro Airport will have a financial effect on 
the airlines. 
In addition, it is clear that the effects of 
leases on airport tenants are distinguishable from their effects 
on the public generally or a significant segment thereof. 
In 
particular, decisions concerning the County's agreement with the 
tenant airlines will primarily affect only the airlines 
involved. 
Thus the key question for analysis is whether 
modifications of the existing agreement with the airlines and/or 
the new agreement to be negotiated will have a material effect 
on Western Airlines. 
The Commission has provided numerical guidelines for the 
determination of a material effect on a business entity: 
of: 
* * * 
(C) 
Current assets or liabilities by the lesser 
1. 
One hundred thousand dollars ($100,000) ~ 
or 
2. 
One half of one percent if the effect is 
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more. 
Current assets are deemed to 
be decreased by 
the amount of any expenses incurred as a result of 
a governmental decision. 
(2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18702 (b) (1) (C). >Y 
(Copy 
enclosed. ) 
Assuming that Western Airlines' annualized gross revenues 
exceed $1 million, a financial effect would have to be $100,000 
to be considered material. 
You indicated to me that Western 
Airlines pays approximately $50,000 to the County for its use of 
Metro Airport under the current Agreement. 
§./ 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702 (b) (1) (A) and (B) are 
not relevant to this situation. 


J. Steven Burris 
February 15, 1984 
page 5 
It does not appear that any modifications of the existing 
Agreement could have a $100,000, or material, effect on Western 
Airlines. 
The modifications would only adjust the fees paid by 
the airlines to compensate for inflation and other changes in 
the cost and revenue picture. 
Thus, Ms. Harrison can 
participate in decisions concerning modifications of the present 
Agreement. 
It is a more difficult question whether Ms. Harrison will be 
able to participate in the negotiations for a new Agreement when 
this Agreement expires. 
Assuming that Western Airlines plans to 
continue doing business in Sacramento, it would first be 
necessary to determine what issues were on the negotiating 
table, and whether the impact on Western Airlines from the 
potential resolutions to any of these issues would be 
significant. 
We understand that at the outset of negotiations 
it may be difficult to determine what the issues will be. 
Based 
on her knowledge and experience, Ms. Harrison should first 
determine what serious proposals from the County and from the 
airlines will probably be on the negotiating table. 
Then, for 
each proposal, if either the difference between the existing 
Agreement and the proposal or the differences between the 
proposals on the same issue on the table amount to a possible 
$100,000 effect on Western Airlines, then Ms. Harrison should 
not participate in, at the minimum, the negotiations on that 
proposal.21 
For example, if the airlines propose what would 
amount to a $50,000 reduction in fees and charges paid by 
Western Airlines, and the County proposes a similar increase in 
the amounts paid, the range between the proposals adds up to 
$100,000, and the decision on that issue would have a material 
financial effect on Western Airlines. 
possible negotiating 
issues that could affect Western Airlines include not only fees 
and other direct charges but also other contract provisions 
(e.g., the amount of space alloted for the airline). 
In any event, the negotiations are a few years off, and 
Ms. Harrison may wish to request our advice again when the 
potential issues in the negotiation for the new Agreement become 
clearer. 
It may be that there are no issues with respect to the 
new Agreement that could have $100,000 effect on Western 
Airlines, and Ms. Harrison's disqualification would not be 
required. 
21 It may be that the proposal is at the center of the 
negotiations, and, as a practical matter, she may have to 
disqualify herself from the entire process. 


J. Steven Burris 
February 15, 1984 
Page 6 
Please feel free to contact me if you would like to discuss 
this letter or if you have any further questions. 
DMF:plh 
Enclosure 
Sincerely,

This is to respond to your questions regarding the discretion 
which is available to F. Susan Harrison, the Deputy Director of 
the Sacramento County Department of Airports in negotiating 
County airline leases. 
It is the policy of the County to operate 
its airport in a self-supporting manner. 
To this end, the County 
negotiates with the airlines to recover its airport costs of 
operation and maintenance, capital expenditures, and reserves for 
capital expenditures. 
These negotiations are conducted openly between the Deputy 
Director and a group consisting of representatives from each of 
the airlines. 
Beginning with the total cost of operation of the 
airport revenue sources are subtracted and the remaining cost is 
recovered from the airlines Revenue sources under the agreement 
consist of income which the County receives from sources such as 
the operation of the hotel and gift shop, car rental concessions, 
taxi concessions, and other miscellaneous sources of income. 
See 
copy of agreement attached. 
The remaining cost is recovered from 
the airlines under two fee categories. 
The first source of cost 
recovery is the landing strip usage fees which are set at a 
certain cost per ton of landing weight which is utilized by the 
airline. 
The second source of funds is the amount of space 
utilized by the airline; fees are charged for such space within 
the terminal on a per square foot basis. 
The total amount of 
such fees plus the other income sources must add up to the amount 
originally set for the total cost of operation of the airport. 


Diane Maura Fishburn 
-2-
January 9, 1983 
The discretion available to the Deputy Director in 
negotiating the agreement is quite limited. 
Under various agree-
ments the County has applied for and recieved aid from the 
Airport Development Air Program under the jurisdiction of the 
Federal Aviation Administration (FAA). 
As a result, the County 
is bound under contract with the FAA to comply with Sponsors 
Assurances. 
Assurance No. 18, copy attached, provides that the 
carriers, including Western Airlines, shall be treated in an 
identical manner. 
The Deputy Director has no discretion to treat 
Western Airlines any differently than any of the other airlines. 
The discretion which is given to the Deputy Director in such 
negotiations is the ability to set the rate for landing fees on a 
tonnage basis and the ability to set the price of the rental 
space on a per square foot basis. 
It should be noted that 
although the existing agreement is for a five year period, the 
agreement is nevertheless subject to modifications on an annual 
basis. 
Such modifications allow for little discretion on the 
part of the Deputy Director as they are narrowly spelled out in a 
formula in the agreement. 
Also enclosed for your information is the Attorney General 
opinion dated May 30, 1975 and the letter requesting advice dated 
June 9, 1983. 
If I can be of further assistance, please let me know. 
JSB:nm 
Enclosures 
Very truly yours, 
L. B. ELAM, County Counsel 
By 
J. ~EVEN BURRIS, Deputy

Subject: 
REQUEST FOR REVIEH OF CONFLICT OF INTEREST STATUS 
As previously discussed with you, I would appreciate your review of 
my duties with the Depar~~ent of Airports to determine if there is 
any conflict of interest with some of my personal interests. 
Those 
areas of my personal life that have any connection whatsoever with 
the department are described below. 
Likewise, I have outlined my 
new duties as Deputy Director for Finance and Administration. 
PERSONAL 
1. 
My husband is employed as a pilot with Western Airlines. 
He 
currently flies as a co-pilot on the Boeing 737 and is based 
in Salt Lake City. He commutes to "'lork in Salt Lake City 
from Sacramento on Western. 
Strictly as a benefit of his job, 
he and I and his parents receive discounted travel on Western 
and on other airlines with whom Western has reciprocal travel 
agreements. 
All Western employees and their families receive 
the same travel benefits. 
I have not and would not accept free 
or discounted travel from any airline as a result of my position 
\'lith the depar~~ent. 
-
2. 
My husband and I own two aircraft. 
One of these is stored in 
one of the 151 County-owned T-hangars at Executive Airport, 
for which we pay the going monthly rental. 
3. 
We purch~~e fuel for our aircraft from the County. 
4. 
We purchase some supplies from and have some services performed 
on or for our aircraft by tenants at Executive Airport. 
5. 
vIe live in a corru':mni ty called Fullertown, which is an airport 
housing area on the east side of Executive Airport off 24th Street. 
The several homes vii thin the Fullertovm community have direct taxi-
i,'lay access-c6-Executive Airport. 
The cOIn:munity~-w-asestablished =-----
least fifteen years ago. 
We purchased an existing home there 
\'li th existing taxi\'lay almost five years ago. 
A former pa::tner in 
our second aircraft built a home and attached hangar in Fnllertown 
about 5-1/2 years ago. 
He stores our second airplane in his 
hangar.

This letter is written to request your opinion regarding a 
potential conflict of interest which has arisen regarding the 
employment of the Deputy Director of Airports of Sacramento 
County. 
The husband of 
Western Airlines. 
such as discounted 
airlines and stock 
the Deputy Director is employed as a pilot for 
In addition to his salary he receives benefits 
travel for his family on Western and other 
ownership in Western Airlines. 
The Deputy Director of Airports is responsible for the 
negotiation of tenant leases at both Sacramento Executive and the 
Sacramento Metropolitan Airports. 
A new five-year airline lease 
negotiated by the predecessor of the present Deputy Director has 
recently gone into effect. 
Such lease includes formulas for 
annual adjustments to the terminal space rental rates and landing 
fees. 
All signatory airlines (Western Airlines is a signatory 
airline) pay the same rates. 
When the present lease expires, the 
Deputy Director will be responsible for negotiating new leases 
with the signatory airlines for recommendation to the Director of 
Airports who 
will in turn recommend the lease for adoption by 
the Board of Supervisors. 
The negotiations with Western Airlines cannot be conducted 
separately from negotiations with the other airlines because all 


Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
December 13, 1983 
the airlines are treated in an identical manner. 
The effect of 
the negotiations is to set charges on all airlines for the use of 
the County facility. 
The amounts of the charges and time over 
which the charges are made are subject to negotiation. 
Western 
Airlines' share of such charges will likely continue to be in 
excess of $50,000.00 on an annual basis. 
Our question is whether the Deputy Director must be 
disqualified from negotiating the new leases with the signatory 
airlines. 
If you need further clarifications or additional 
information, please let me know. 
JSB~bjh 
Very truly yours, 
L. B. ELAM

The County policy on the operation of Metro Airport is that 
it must be self-supporting. 
Accordingly, in negotiating the 
Agreement, the Deputy Director (Ms. Harrison) begins with the 
total cost of the airport operation, subtracts the amounts from 
revenue sources other than the airlines,1/ and arrives at the 
amount which must be recovered from the airlines. 
The airlines 
are charged for their use of the airport in two ways. 
One 
charge is the landing strip usage fee which is set at a certain 
l/ Both of these questions raise the issue of the 
possible application of Section 1090. 
This office renders 
advice only on the provisions of the Political Reform Act;
sections.analysis:
As you know, Section 87100 prohibits a public official from· 
making or participating in governmental decisions in which the 
official has a financial interest in the outcome. 
Section 87103 
defines a financial interest as a reasonably foreseeable 
material financial effect on: 
(a) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect investment worth more 
than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(b) 
Any real property in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect interest worth more 
than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than loans by a 
commercial lending institution in the regular course of 
business on terms available to the public without 
regard to official status, aggregating two hundred 
fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, 
received by or promised to the public official within 
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. 
(d) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. 
(Section 87103 (a) - (d) .) 
Investments include investments of a spouse, and income includes 
income to a spouse. 
Sections 82030 and 82034. !Thus, Western 
Airlines is a source of income to Ms. Harrison and a business 


J. Steven Burris 
February 15, 1984  
entity in which she has an investment. 
Accordingly, 
Ms. Harrison may not make or participate in decisions which 
could have a material financial effect on Western Airlines, 
distinguishable from their effect on the public generally. 
It is reasonably foreseeable that decisions concerning the 
airline leases at Metro Airport will have a financial effect on 
the airlines. 
In addition, it is clear that the effects of 
leases on airport tenants are distinguishable from their effects 
on the public generally or a significant segment thereof. 
In 
particular, decisions concerning the County's agreement with the 
tenant airlines will primarily affect only the airlines 
involved. 
Thus the key question for analysis is whether 
modifications of the existing agreement with the airlines and/or 
the new agreement to be negotiated will have a material effect 
on Western Airlines. 
The Commission has provided numerical guidelines for the 
determination of a material effect on a business entity:

sections.question_synthetic: Whether F. Susan Harrison, Deputy Director of the Sacramento County Department of Airports, has a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act when negotiating airline operating agreements at Sacramento Metropolitan Airport, given her husband's employment by Western Airlines and stock holdings in the company.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Ms. Harrison may participate in routine modifications of the existing airline agreement since they would not have a material ($100,000+) financial effect on Western Airlines. However, for future negotiations of a new agreement, she must evaluate each proposal to determine if it could have a $100,000 or greater impact on Western Airlines; if so, she must disqualify herself from those specific negotiations.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr, old; LLM: Clear advice letter with well-defined question and conclusion sections. Includes factual background, analysis of Political Reform Act provisions (Sections 87100, 87103), and materiality guidelines from administrative code. Secondary document included is incoming request letter from County Counsel dated January 9, 1984.

QUESTION: s concerning Section 1090 should be directed to the 
Attorney General's office. 
i/ The County receives aid from the Airport Development 
Air Program under the jurisdiction of the Federal Aviation 
Administration. 
A condition on receiving this aid is that all 
of the carriers must be treated alike in operation and lease 
agreements. 
i/ Other revenue sources include the hotel ,and gift shop, 
car rental and taxi concessions and other miscellaneous sources. 


J. Steven Burris 
February 15, 1984  
cost per ton of landing weight utilized by the airline. 
The 
other charge is a cost per square foot of space used by the 
airline in the terminal. 
The total which is charged the 
airlines must add up with the other revenues to the amount 
originally set as the total cost for airport operations. 
Even 
though this formula is used, there is still discretion in the 
setting of the charges and in spelling out the other terms of 
the Agreement. 
In addition to participating in the negotiations for the 
Agreement itself, the Deputy Director is responsible for annual 
modifications of the Agreement. 
These modifications to the 
charges are done pursuant to a formula in the Agreement.

CONCLUSION: Ms. Harrison can participate in decisions concerning modifications of the present Agreement. It is a more difficult question whether Ms. Harrison will be able to participate in the negotiations for a new Agreement when this Agreement expires. For each proposal being negotiated, if either the difference between the existing Agreement and the proposal or the differences between proposals on the same issue amount to a possible $100,000 effect on Western Airlines, then Ms. Harrison should not participate in negotiations on that proposal.

Susan Harrison, the Deputy Director of the Sacramento County Department of Airports, concerning the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.ll FACTUAL BACKGROUND21 Ms. Harrison's husband is employed as a pilot by Western Airlines. As a benefit of his job, Ms. Harrison and her husband receive discounted airline travel on Western and other airlines. In addition, her husband participates in an employee stock sharing program. Ms. Harrison'S job duties as the Deputy Director for Finance and Administration include: 1. Preparing the Department's budget. 2. Analyzing and recommending changes to airport rates and charges (e.g., rates for T-hangars and tie-downs). 11 California Government Code Sections 81000-91014. All statutory references are to the California Government Code. 11 These facts come from our telephone conversations, Ms. Harrison's memo to your office dated June 9, 1983, your letters to me dated December 13, 1983 and January 9, 1984, and the materials included with your letter. J. Steven Burris February 15, 1984 Page 2 3. Negotiating tenant leases at both Sacramento Metropolitan and Executive Airports. 4. Administering the air trade development program which is a marketing program designed to promote use of Me tr 0 Airport •. My understanding is that you have already advised Ms. Harrison with respect to the setting of airport rates and charges. Your specific question to us is whether Ms. Harrison would have a conflict of interest in negotiating the airline leases at Sacramento Metropolitan Airport.l/ The County of Sacramento currently has an "Airline Operating Agreement and Terminal Building Lease" (the "Agreement") with the airlines for use of Sacramento Metropolitan Airport {"Metro Airport"}. This Agreement was negotiated by Ms. Harrison's predecessor and became effective in June 1983; it is a five-year Agreement. The negotiations for the Agreement are conducted between the Deputy Director and a group of airline representatives. The County is required to treat the airlines in an identical manner.if The County policy on the operation of Metro Airport is that it must be self-supporting. Accordingly, in negotiating the Agreement, the Deputy Director (Ms. Harrison) begins with the total cost of the airport operation, subtracts the amounts from revenue sources other than the airlines,1/ and arrives at the amount which must be recovered from the airlines. The airlines are charged for their use of the airport in two ways. One charge is the landing strip usage fee which is set at a certain l/ Both of these questions raise the issue of the possible application of Section 1090. This office renders advice
embedding.summary: FPPC advises on whether Deputy Director F. Susan Harrison has a conflict of interest negotiating airline leases at Sacramento Metropolitan Airport given her husband's employment and stock holdings with Western Airlines. The Commission concludes she may participate in routine modifications but must disqualify herself from future negotiations on any proposals that could have a material ($100,000+) financial effect on Western Airlines.
